findings.title: Assumption 3 threshold uses ‖b̂‖ but first-best condition uses ‖b̂‖²
findings.snippet:
Prose (Sec. 4): "when the budget is large enough—that is, C ≥ ‖b̂‖²"
Assumption 3: "Either w < 0 and C < ‖b̂‖, or w > 0."
findings.explanation: The narrative argument directly preceding Assumption 3 establishes that the first-best is achievable when C ≥ ‖b̂‖². This follows from the budget constraint being quadratic: the reformulated problem (IT) rewrites the constraint as Σ_ℓ b̂²_ℓ x²_ℓ ≤ C, which equals ‖y‖² ≤ C; setting b = 0 requires ‖y‖² = ‖b̂‖². Assumption 3 then attempts to exclude this first-best case for w < 0, but writes C < ‖b̂‖ (the unsquared norm) rather than C < ‖b̂‖². The two conditions are not equivalent—they agree only when ‖b̂‖ = 1—so depending on the scale of b̂, Assumption 3 either fails to exclude the first-best region (‖b̂‖ < 1) or imposes a strictly tighter restriction than necessary (‖b̂‖ > 1). As a formal assumption underpinning Theorem 1, this appears to be a missing exponent.
findings.fix: Change Assumption 3 to read: "Either w < 0 and C < ‖b̂‖², or w > 0." This makes the formal assumption consistent with the prose condition C ≥ ‖b̂‖² and with the quadratic budget constraint in problem (IT).

findings.title: Welfare function W uses comma in Section 4 but semicolon in Section 5
findings.snippet:
Sec. 4: "W(b, G) = w · (a*)ᵀ a*"
Sec. 5: "choose r.v. B to maximize E[W(b; G)]" and "E[W(b; G)] = w E[(a*)ᵀ a*]"
findings.explanation: Both sections refer to the same welfare function, but Section 4 writes it as W(b, G) (comma-separated arguments) while Section 5 consistently writes it as W(b; G) (semicolon-separated). In applied-math conventions, the semicolon notation often distinguishes a parameter from an argument; using both styles for the same object without declaration introduces ambiguity and makes cross-section reading harder. No convention switch is declared anywhere in either section.
findings.fix: Standardise to one notation throughout. The comma form W(b, G) is more common in the game-theory literature and already established earlier in the paper; adopt it uniformly in Section 5 as well (or vice versa, with an explicit declaration).

findings.title: Superscript ordering (·ᵀ)* in Section 5 welfare formula inconsistent with (·*)ᵀ convention
findings.snippet:
Sec. 5 welfare formula middle step: "w E[ (ā^⊤)* (ā*) ]"
Sec. 4 convention: "(a*)ᵀ a*" and "ā*_ℓ = √α_ℓ b̄_ℓ"
findings.explanation: Throughout Section 4, the equilibrium action vector in the principal-component basis is written as ā* (underlined bold, star on the vector), and the inner product is (a*)ᵀ a*. In the intermediate step of the welfare decomposition in Section 5, the same quantity is written as (ā^⊤)* (ā*), i.e. the star is applied after the transpose on the first factor rather than before it. Besides being typographically awkward, this inconsistently suggests that the transposition and the equilibrium-selection operations are applied in different orders, which could confuse readers trying to follow the chain of equalities.
findings.fix: Replace (ā^⊤)*(ā*) with (ā*)^⊤(ā*) to match the established convention from Section 4 and make the chain of equalities self-consistent.
